name: Algo Lab
description: Hands-on algorithmic trading workflow — data pipelines, backtest hygiene, prop rules as code, journaling, strategy specs, and review discipline.

nodes.name: Data Pipeline: Tick → Bar → Parquet
nodes.description: Build a reproducible market-data pipeline: ingest raw ticks, aggregate OHLCV bars, and persist to Parquet for backtests and research.
keyConcepts: Data quality checks (gaps, outliers, timezone alignment)
nodes.whyItMatters: Garbage in, garbage out. Every backtest, journal stat, and coach review depends on clean, replayable data. A tick→bar→Parquet pipeline is the foundation of algo trading — without it you're curve-fitting on screenshots.
practiceProblems: Design a schema for 1-minute ES bars stored in Parquet. What columns do you need and why?
You receive duplicate ticks with the same exchange timestamp. How do you dedupe without losing legitimate trades?
A bar file has a 45-minute gap during RTH. List 3 checks to decide if it's a data bug or a halted market.
Convert 10M ticks to 1-min bars. Estimate memory if you load all ticks vs stream aggregation.

resources.title: Apache Parquet Documentation
resources.whyHelpful: Official Parquet format docs — understand columnar storage, compression, and why it's the standard for quant datasets.

resources.title: Pandas: Working with Time Series
resources.whyHelpful: Resampling ticks to bars, timezone handling, and gap detection — the Python primitives every pipeline uses.

resources.title: Databento: Market Data Formats
resources.whyHelpful: Clear comparison of tick, MBP, and OHLCV formats from a modern data vendor. Helps you pick the right granularity.

quiz.question: Why store research datasets as Parquet instead of CSV?
options: Parquet is human-readable in any text editor
Columnar compression, typed schema, and fast partial reads
quiz.explanation: Parquet stores columns separately with compression and embedded types. You can read only the columns you need (e.g., close + volume) without loading the whole file — critical for large tick archives.

quiz.question: When aggregating ticks to 1-minute bars, which price represents the 'open' of the bar?
options: The last tick before the minute boundary
The first tick at or after the minute boundary
The midpoint of bid and ask at minute start
The volume-weighted average of all ticks
quiz.explanation: Standard OHLCV convention: open = first trade in the interval, close = last trade, high/low = extrema. Consistency matters more than the exact rule — document yours.

quiz.question: You partition Parquet files by trading date. What is the main benefit?
options: Smaller individual files and faster date-range queries
quiz.explanation: Date partitioning lets backtests load only the days they need. It also keeps file sizes manageable and makes incremental daily updates trivial.

workedExamples.problem: You have CSV tick files: timestamp, price, size. Build a 5-minute bar for ES on 2024-01-15. Show open, high, low, close, volume.
workedExamples.solution: Group ticks into 5-min buckets (floor timestamp to 5-min boundary). open = first price in bucket, high = max(price), low = min(price), close = last price, volume = sum(size). Write one row per bucket to Parquet with columns: symbol, bar_start, open, high, low, close, volume.
workedExamples.explanation: This is the minimal viable bar builder. In production, handle session boundaries (RTH vs ETH), partial bars at session open, and timezone (America/Chicago for CME).

workedExamples.problem: Your pipeline ingests 2 duplicate ticks (same ts, price, size). Bars look identical with or without dupes. Why still dedupe?
workedExamples.solution: Volume would double-count if you sum size blindly. Dedupe key: (exchange_ts, price, size, trade_id) or vendor sequence number. Even if OHLC is unchanged, volume-based indicators (VWAP, volume profile) will lie.
workedExamples.explanation: OHLC can hide volume errors. Always validate volume separately — it's the first place duplicate feeds corrupt your research.

commonMistakes: Mixing timezones — store everything in UTC, convert only for display.
Building bars on calendar minutes instead of session-aware boundaries for futures.
Skipping deduplication because OHLC 'looks fine' — volume silently doubles.
Writing one giant Parquet file instead of partitioning by date — backtests become painfully slow.

nodes.name: Backtest Hygiene: Lookahead, Fees, Slippage
nodes.description: Stress-test backtests for lookahead bias, realistic transaction costs, and slippage models. A pretty equity curve means nothing if the simulation cheats.
keyConcepts: Lookahead bias and peeking at future data
Commission, exchange fees, and round-turn cost
Slippage models: fixed ticks, proportional, volume-based
nodes.whyItMatters: Most 'profitable' backtests die on the first live trade. Lookahead, zero fees, and perfect fills are the three lies that kill accounts. Hygiene isn't optional — it's the difference between research and fantasy.
practiceProblems: Your strategy uses today's close to generate today's entry signal. Name the bias and fix it.
ES round-turn costs $4.50 all-in. A scalper averages 8 trades/day. What's the monthly fee drag on 20 trading days?
Compare fixed 1-tick slippage vs 0.1% proportional slippage on a $2-wide spread instrument.
List 3 ways walk-forward testing catches overfitting that a single backtest hides.

resources.title: QuantStart: Backtesting Pitfalls
resources.whyHelpful: Classic walkthrough of lookahead, survivorship, and overfitting traps — still the best sanity checklist.

resources.title: Investopedia: Look-Ahead Bias
resources.whyHelpful: Short, precise definition with examples. Use when reviewing any signal that uses same-bar data.

resources.title: Zipline / Backtrader Slippage Docs
resources.whyHelpful: Shows how mainstream backtest engines model slippage — compare your assumptions to industry defaults.

quiz.question: You backtest with a 20-day SMA calculated using the current bar's close for the entry decision on that same bar. This is:
options: Valid — SMAs are lagging by design
Lookahead bias — you used information not known at decision time
quiz.explanation: If you decide at bar open but the signal uses bar close, you're peeking. Shift signals by one bar or decide at bar close explicitly — and model execution delay.

quiz.question: A strategy shows 60% win rate and 1.5R average win with zero fees. After adding $5 round-turn and 1-tick slippage, expectancy turns negative. What happened?
options: The strategy was never real — edge was smaller than friction
Win rate should increase with fees
quiz.explanation: Small edges get eaten by friction. Scalping and high-frequency strategies are especially fee-sensitive. Always net out costs before celebrating.

quiz.question: Walk-forward testing primarily helps you detect:
options: Overfitting to a specific historical window
quiz.explanation: Walk-forward trains on window A, tests on B, rolls forward. If performance collapses out-of-sample, you curve-fit — not discovered edge.

workedExamples.problem: Strategy: buy when close > 50 SMA, sell when close < 50 SMA. Backtest shows 40% CAGR. Audit for lookahead.
workedExamples.solution: Bug: signal uses same-bar close but simulates fill at close — you knew the close before 'deciding.' Fix: compute SMA on prior bars only; enter next bar open with 1-tick slippage. Re-run — CAGR often drops 50%+.
workedExamples.explanation: Same-bar signal+fill is the #1 backtest bug. Always ask: 'At the moment I decide, what do I actually know?'

workedExamples.problem: NQ scalper: 12 round turns/day, $4.80 all-in per RT, avg gross winner $18, avg gross loser $12, 55% win rate. Is it viable before slippage?
workedExamples.solution: Expectancy gross = 0.55×18 − 0.45×12 = 9.9 − 5.4 = $4.50/trade. Fees = 12 × $4.80 = $57.60/day. Gross P&L ≈ 12 × $4.50 = $54/day. Net ≈ −$3.60/day before slippage. Not viable — edge < friction.
workedExamples.explanation: Always subtract round-turn costs per trade, not per day lump sum. High trade count amplifies fee drag exponentially.

commonMistakes: Using adjusted prices without understanding corporate actions (less common in futures, deadly in equities).
Optimizing parameters on the full dataset then reporting in-sample results as 'live expected.'
Assuming limit orders always fill at your price — model partial fills and miss rates.
Ignoring slippage during volatile opens when your strategy actually trades.

nodes.name: Expectancy & Position Sizing
nodes.description: Translate win rate and payoff ratio into expectancy, then size positions so one bad streak doesn't end the account. Math beats gut feel.
keyConcepts: Expectancy: E = (Win% × Avg Win) − (Loss% × Avg Loss)
Risk per trade as % of account (1R framework)
Fixed fractional vs fixed dollar sizing
Kelly criterion (and why half-Kelly is safer)
Drawdown math and consecutive loss streaks
nodes.whyItMatters: Positive expectancy with wrong sizing still blows up. Prop firms and survival both come down to: how much do you risk when you're wrong? This lab turns t3 theory into executable sizing rules.
practiceProblems: Win rate 45%, avg win $200, avg loss $100. Calculate expectancy per trade.
Account $25,000, risk 1% per trade, stop is 10 ticks on ES ($12.50/tick). How many contracts?
6 consecutive losses at 1% risk — what is account drawdown? At 3% risk?
Two uncorrelated strategies each risk 1%. Is total portfolio heat 2%? Explain.

resources.title: Van Tharp: Position Sizing
resources.whyHelpful: The classic R-multiple framework — expectancy and sizing as one system, not separate topics.

resources.title: Investopedia: Kelly Criterion
resources.whyHelpful: Mathematical optimal bet sizing — know when Kelly applies and when it over-bets.

resources.title: Khan Academy: Expected Value
resources.whyHelpful: Foundational EV math that expectancy builds on — quick refresher if probability rust is showing.

quiz.question: Strategy: 40% win rate, avg win $300, avg loss $150. Expectancy per trade?
$50k account, 1% risk ($500), stop distance $250 per contract. Max contracts?
quiz.explanation: Contracts = risk dollars / stop dollars = 500 / 250 = 2. Never round up — fractional risk beats oversizing.

quiz.question: Why do prop traders often use half-Kelly or less instead of full Kelly?
options: Full Kelly maximizes long-run growth but produces brutal drawdowns; estimates are noisy
quiz.explanation: Kelly assumes you know true win rate and payoff exactly. You don't. Full Kelly over-bets on estimation error; half-Kelly sacrifices peak growth for survivability.

workedExamples.problem: 40% WR, +$300 / −$150. Expectancy? With $50k and 1% risk, stop $150/contract — size?
workedExamples.explanation: Positive expectancy doesn't mean every trade wins — it means the distribution pays you over many trades. Sizing ensures one loss ≠ one blown account.

workedExamples.problem: 10-trade losing streak at 1% risk per trade. Approximate drawdown?
workedExamples.solution: Compounded: (0.99)^10 ≈ 0.904 → ~9.6% drawdown. Linear approximation: 10%. At 2% risk: (0.98)^10 ≈ 18.3%. Streaks happen — size for them.
workedExamples.explanation: Traders underestimate streak length. At 40% win rate, 10 losses in a row is rare but inevitable over hundreds of trades.

commonMistakes: Sizing based on conviction instead of stop distance — bigger bets on 'sure things' is how accounts die.
Ignoring correlation when running multiple strategies — heat stacks.
Using full Kelly with backtested stats treated as gospel.
Moving stops wider after entry to avoid a loss — changes your R and invalidates sizing.

nodes.name: Prop Firm Rules as Code
nodes.description: Encode prop-firm constraints — daily loss limits, trailing drawdown, consistency rules — as executable guards that block bad orders before they reach the market.
keyConcepts: Daily loss limit (DLL) and max trailing drawdown
Consistency / payout rules (% of best day)
Minimum trading days and inactivity rules
nodes.whyItMatters: Blowing a funded account over one revenge trade is a code problem, not a psychology lecture. When rules live in code — not sticky notes — the platform stops you before the firm does.
practiceProblems: Account $50k, max trailing DD $2,500, high-water $52,100, current equity $50,000. How much room left?
Best day profit $1,200, consistency rule: no single day > 40% of total profit. Total profit $4,000. Is best day compliant?
Design a pre-trade guard: max 3 contracts ES if unrealized day P&L < −$800.
Write pseudocode for a DLL hard stop that flattens and locks trading until next session.

resources.title: Apex Trader Funding: Rule Overview
resources.whyHelpful: Real prop firm rule sheet — use as a template for which constraints to encode first.

resources.title: Topstep: Trading Combine Rules
resources.whyHelpful: Another major firm's rule structure — compare trailing drawdown vs static max loss implementations.

resources.title: Martin Fowler: Guard Clause Pattern
resources.whyHelpful: Software pattern for early-exit validation — exactly how pre-trade rule checks should be structured.

quiz.question: Trailing drawdown $3,000, high-water mark $105,000. Minimum allowed equity?
quiz.explanation: Floor = HWM − trailing DD = 105,000 − 3,000 = $102,000. The limit ratchets up with new highs.

quiz.question: Why implement prop rules in code instead of relying on discipline?
options: Emotion bypasses discipline in real time; guards enforce rules when cortisol is high
Rules don't apply during news events
quiz.explanation: The revenge-trade moment is exactly when humans override rules. Automated guards remove the option.

quiz.question: A soft warning vs hard block: which is appropriate for hitting 80% of daily loss limit?
options: Hard block — always stop all trading
Soft warning first, hard block at 100% of limit
Soft warning only after account blown
quiz.explanation: Progressive enforcement: warn at 80%, block new risk at 100%, optionally flatten. Gives awareness without surprise lockouts on minor breaches.

workedExamples.problem: HWM $52,100, trailing DD $2,500, current equity $50,000. Room before breach?
workedExamples.solution: Floor = 52,100 − 2,500 = $49,600. Current $50,000 → $400 buffer. One bad ES trade (~$500–$1000) breaches. Guard should block new entries now.
workedExamples.explanation: Prop traders focus on distance to breach, not P&L vanity. Encode 'buffer < X → no new trades' as a hard rule.

workedExamples.problem: Pseudocode: daily loss limit hard stop at −$1,000 day P&L.
workedExamples.solution: on_order(order): if day_pnl <= -1000: reject(order, 'DLL breached'); flatten_all(); lock_until(next_session). on_fill: update day_pnl; if day_pnl <= -800: warn('80% DLL'); if day_pnl <= -1000: flatten_all(); lock.
workedExamples.explanation: Check on every order and every fill. Flatten + lock prevents the 'one more trade to get it back' spiral.

commonMistakes: Calculating drawdown on unrealized peaks incorrectly — use firm-defined HWM rules exactly.
Hard blocking without flatten logic — open positions can still breach after the block.
Ignoring consistency rules until payout day — track daily profit % from trade one.
Different rule implementations between sim and live — test guards in paper first.

nodes.name: Journal Protocol: Thesis Before Click
nodes.description: Mandatory pre-trade thesis: setup, invalidation, target, and size — logged before order entry. No thesis, no click.
keyConcepts: Setup taxonomy (breakout, mean reversion, news fade)
nodes.whyItMatters: Post-hoc narratives lie. A thesis-before-click protocol creates auditable evidence of your edge — and exposes when you're gambling. Coaches and future-you can only review what you wrote down in advance.
practiceProblems: Write a complete pre-trade thesis for a long ES breakout above overnight high.
You didn't write a thesis but took the trade. How should the journal flag it?
Tag 5 recent trades with setup type and emotional state. What pattern emerges?
Design 4 required fields that must be filled before order submission in your stack.

resources.title: Trading Journal Best Practices (Edgewonk)
resources.whyHelpful: Structured journaling workflow — what to capture and how to review weekly.

resources.title: Brett Steenbarger: Trading Psychology
resources.whyHelpful: Research-backed perspective on process vs outcome — supports thesis-first discipline.

resources.title: Learn v2 Trading Lab Journal
resources.whyHelpful: Connect journal protocol to the local tradingv1 backend when running — same fields, live integration.

quiz.question: What is the main purpose of a pre-trade thesis?
options: Create an auditable plan before outcome bias kicks in
quiz.explanation: Once you know the result, your brain rewrites the story. Pre-trade logs capture intent — the only fair input for coach review.

quiz.question: Minimum viable thesis fields before click:
quiz.explanation: Setup = why now. Invalidation = when you're wrong. Target = planned R. Size = risk alignment. Four fields, 30 seconds, saves accounts.

quiz.question: A 'revenge' emotional tag on 40% of trades this week suggests:
options: Process breakdown — review rules and consider cooldown guards
quiz.explanation: Emotional tagging makes invisible patterns visible. High revenge % → enforce DLL guards (lab4) and mandatory cooldown after losses.

workedExamples.problem: Long ES: broke overnight high 5245, pullback held 5242, targeting 5255, stop 5238, 2 contracts (1% risk). Write thesis.
workedExamples.solution: Setup: ORB continuation — cleared ONH with volume, retest held. Thesis: momentum into RTH balance. Invalidation: 5-min close < 5238 (below retest low). Target: 5255 (prior week VAH). Size: 2 ES = $500 risk vs $50k (1%). State: calm, planned A+ setup.
workedExamples.explanation: Specific levels and labels let you grade the trade honestly later — did you follow plan or move stop?

workedExamples.problem: Trader clicks buy with empty thesis field. System allows it. Fix?
workedExamples.solution: Soft launch: warn + require 'override reason' for 2 weeks. Hard launch: block order API until thesis JSON validates (all required fields non-empty). Log overrides separately for coach review.
workedExamples.explanation: Gradual enforcement reduces rebellion; hard blocks enforce culture. Never allow silent empty theses in steady state.

commonMistakes: Writing thesis after the fill — outcome bias makes this worthless.
Vague invalidation ('if it feels wrong') — must be a level or time rule.
Skipping emotional tags on winners — overconfidence patterns hide there too.
Journal disconnected from order flow — if it's not in the execution path, it won't happen.

nodes.name: Strategy Spec Template (STRATEGY.md)
nodes.description: Document strategies in a standard STRATEGY.md: thesis, universe, signals, risk, parameters, and failure modes — the contract between research and implementation.
keyConcepts: STRATEGY.md sections: thesis, universe, signals, execution, risk, params
Regime filters and when NOT to trade
Link spec → backtest config → live config
nodes.whyItMatters: Undocumented strategies drift. STRATEGY.md is the single source of truth — what you think you're trading, what code actually does, and when to pull the plug. Lab2 hygiene and lab5 journal both reference this doc.
practiceProblems: Outline STRATEGY.md for a 20/50 SMA cross on NQ with ADX filter > 25.
Your live bot diverged from backtest — which spec sections do you diff first?
Write kill criteria: 3 conditions that pause the strategy automatically.
Parameter 'lookback=14' changed to 12 mid-month. Where must that be logged?

resources.title: GitHub: Strategy Spec Example (QuantConnect)
resources.whyHelpful: Framework thinking — universe, alpha, portfolio, execution — maps cleanly to STRATEGY.md sections.

resources.title: Write the Docs: README-driven Development
resources.whyHelpful: Document-first discipline — spec before code, same ethos as thesis before click.

resources.title: tradingv1 STRATEGY.md (local repo)
resources.whyHelpful: Your implementation repo should host one STRATEGY.md per strategy — link spec hashes to backtest run IDs for traceability.

quiz.question: Which STRATEGY.md section defines when the strategy must NOT trade?
quiz.explanation: Regime filters (low vol, news blackout, trendless ADX) prevent trading when edge doesn't apply — as important as entry rules.

quiz.question: Kill criteria belong in the spec because:
options: They pre-commit exit-from-strategy rules before drawdown pain
quiz.explanation: Kill criteria (e.g., 20-trade rolling expectancy < 0) are decided calmly in the spec — not after a 15% drawdown when you're rationalizing.

quiz.question: Spec says 'enter next bar open'; code enters same bar close. This is a:
options: Spec-implementation mismatch — likely lookahead in backtest
quiz.explanation: Spec and code must match bar-by-bar. Mismatch here recreates lab2 lookahead bugs — diff spec vs backtest config religiously.

workedExamples.problem: Draft STRATEGY.md skeleton for SMA cross strategy.
workedExamples.solution:
# ES Trend Follow v1
## Thesis
Momentum persists in RTH when ADX>25.
## Universe
ES continuous, RTH only.
## Signals
Long: 20 SMA > 50 SMA and ADX>25. Flat: cross down.
## Execution
Next bar open, 1-tick slippage.
## Risk
1% per trade, stop = 1.5× ATR(14).
## Params
SMA 20/50, ADX 25, ATR 14 — frozen 2024-06-01.
## Kill
Rolling 30-trade expectancy < 0 → pause.
workedExamples.explanation: Each section answers one question. A new developer (or future you) can implement or audit without oral tradition.

workedExamples.problem: Live P&L diverges from backtest. Audit checklist from STRATEGY.md.
workedExamples.solution: 1) Universe — same symbols/session? 2) Signals — same bar timing? 3) Execution — slippage/fees match? 4) Params — unchanged since freeze date? 5) Data — same Parquet pipeline (lab1)? Log diff for each.
workedExamples.explanation: Spec-driven debugging beats random tweaking. Most divergences are spec-implementation or data pipeline gaps.

commonMistakes: Spec in someone's head — code becomes the accidental spec.
No parameter freeze date — endless tweaking erases statistical validity.
Missing no-trade regimes — strategy trades 24/7 when edge is RTH-only.
No link from spec version to backtest run ID — can't reproduce results.

nodes.name: Recording & Rithmic (When Creds Exist)
nodes.description: Wire live and sim execution to Rithmic when credentials are available: order events, fills, and session recording for replay and coach review.
keyConcepts: Rithmic API / R | Trader protocol basics
Order lifecycle events (submit, ack, fill, cancel)
nodes.whyItMatters: Paper trading without recording is practice without feedback. When Rithmic creds exist, every fill becomes data — replay mistakes, verify journal theses against tape, and close the loop between lab and market.
practiceProblems: List environment variables for Rithmic creds — what must NEVER be committed?
Design event schema: order_id, ts, side, qty, price, state.
Sim fill differs from live by 2 ticks — what parity checks run before go-live?
Session recording crashed mid-day. How do you reconcile journal vs exchange fills?

resources.title: Rithmic: API Documentation
resources.whyHelpful: Official entry point for Rithmic connectivity — protocols, SDK options, and vendor requirements.

resources.title: 12 Factor App: Config
resources.whyHelpful: Store creds in environment, not repo — mandatory for any trading stack.

resources.title: Learn v2 Trading Lab
resources.whyHelpful: Health check and last backtest card — extend with Rithmic session status when tradingv1 exposes it.

quiz.question: Where should Rithmic username/password live?
options: Environment variables or secret manager — never git
quiz.explanation: Credentials in git = eventual leak. Use .env locally (gitignored), secret manager in prod. Rotate if ever exposed.

quiz.question: Why record order lifecycle events, not just fills?
options: Partial fills, cancels, and rejects explain slippage and bugs
quiz.explanation: A missing fill might be a rejected order, partial, or network drop. Full lifecycle debugs execution issues fills alone hide.

quiz.question: Before enabling live Rithmic with real capital, minimum gate:
options: Sim session with recorded events matching journal + prop guards passing
quiz.explanation: Sim with recording proves integration: thesis journal, rule guards, and event pipeline all work — backtest alone doesn't test execution path.

workedExamples.problem: Event log: SUBMIT buy 2 ES @ MKT → ACK → PARTIAL 1 @ 5245.25 → FILL 1 @ 5245.50. Reconstruct position.
workedExamples.solution: Long 2 ES intended; 1 filled 5245.25, 1 at 5245.50; avg entry 5245.375 if no prior position. Check: partial fill handling in sizing — still within 1% risk?
workedExamples.explanation: Partial fills break naive 'all or nothing' assumptions. Recording lets you verify bot handled remainders correctly.

workedExamples.problem: Creds in .env.example committed with placeholder values — safe?
workedExamples.solution: Placeholder names OK (RITHMIC_USER=changeme); never real values. .env gitignored; CI uses secret store. Add pre-commit hook scanning for rithmic password patterns.
workedExamples.explanation: Example files teach shape; real secrets stay local. One accidental commit = rotate creds immediately.

commonMistakes: Skipping sim recording — live becomes the first integration test.
Same creds for dev and prod — one bug flattens real account.
Recording only P&L snapshots — lose bar-by-bar context for coach replay.
No health check when creds missing — fail loudly, don't silently paper-trade wrong account.

nodes.name: Review Discipline & Coach Bundle
nodes.description: Weekly review ritual plus exportable coach bundle: thesis logs, rule breaches, equity curve, and tagged mistakes — one package for accountability.
keyConcepts: Weekly review agenda (stats, process, one fix)
Coach bundle contents: trades, theses, breaches, notes
One actionable fix per week (not ten)
nodes.whyItMatters: Learning without review is repetition without improvement. The coach bundle turns eight labs into a feedback loop — data pipeline through execution, summarized for the person who holds you accountable.
practiceProblems: List 5 sections in a weekly coach bundle export.
Grade a trade: +$400 P&L but no pre-trade thesis. Process grade?
You found 3 problems in review. Which one do you fix first?
Design a 30-minute weekly review agenda with time boxes.

resources.title: Deliberate Practice (Ericsson summary)
resources.whyHelpful: Expertise comes from feedback loops, not hours — weekly review is deliberate practice for trading.

resources.title: Atomic Habits: Reflection & Review
resources.whyHelpful: Annual/monthly/weekly review cadence — steal the structure for trading process.

resources.title: Learn v2 Stats & Review pages
resources.whyHelpful: Use in-app stats and review queue as the starting point for coach bundle assembly.

quiz.question: Process grade vs outcome grade: +$500 trade with moved stop and no thesis.
options: F process — violated journal and risk protocol regardless of P&L
quiz.explanation: Positive P&L with broken process teaches bad habits. Coach review grades process; outcomes average out over sample size.

quiz.question: Coach bundle should include:
options: Theses, rule events, equity curve, tagged mistakes, weekly note
quiz.explanation: Bundle = full honest snapshot. Breaches and losing theses are the highest-signal content for improvement.

quiz.question: One fix per week principle exists because:
options: Humans can reliably change 10 habits at once
Focus beats scatter — one process change gets measured
Bugs can only be fixed on weekends
quiz.explanation: Ten fixes = zero fixes. Pick the highest-leverage process gap (e.g., empty theses), measure next week, iterate.

workedExamples.problem: Build coach bundle outline for week of 2024-06-10.
workedExamples.solution: 1) Summary: 18 trades, 11 theses, 3 breaches, +$420 net. 2) Equity curve PNG. 3) CSV: trades + thesis fields. 4) Rule log: DLL warn Tue 2:15pm. 5) Tags: 4× FOMO. 6) Coach note: 'Focus — no trade without thesis; set hard block.' 7) One fix: enable thesis gate (lab5).
workedExamples.explanation: Structured bundle saves coach time and forces you to see patterns — FOMO tag clustering is the actionable insight, not the $420.

workedExamples.solution: 0–5: Pull bundle stats. 5–12: Grade 3 random trades (process). 12–18: Rule breaches + emotional tags. 18–25: One fix commitment. 25–30: Schedule next week + SR review queue.
workedExamples.explanation: Time boxes prevent review becoming rumination. Process grading on random sample beats cherry-picking winners.

commonMistakes: Reviewing only P&L — ignores process drift until blowup.
Sending coach 200 trades unfiltered — bundle must curate and summarize.
Ten improvement goals — pick one.
Skipping review when profitable — overconfidence season is when discipline erodes.
